schema_version: v0 — component-charter shape; migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
what: THE USER-FACING COMMAND RUNNER: a `bin/` at the repo root with a single dumb dispatcher, `cairn`, so Akien invokes any Cairn facility by `cairn <name> <args...>` — which resolves to `bin/cmd/<name>` and execs it, argv verbatim, exit code intact. UU's `uu $1 $2 ...` pattern, Cairn's name. The dispatcher owns NO logic of what it runs (Law 6 — each facility owns itself); it resolves a name and hands off, fails LOUD on an unknown name (CP1), and adds nothing to stdout on the success path. A new verb is a new executable in `bin/cmd/` — no edit to the dispatcher. First verb: `bin/cmd/compile` (the intentions-model-compiler's hand-crank). The full name `cairn` lives in git; any 2-letter shorthand is a PERSONAL shell alias in dotfiles (instance-space), never in this shared tree.
why: Founding intention — get Akien out of hand-wiring the system: a named front door beats a remembered path, and it is the seam every later `cairn <verb>` (compile, scan, intent, ...) hangs off. Putting the FULL name in git and leaving the cramped shorthand to a personal alias keeps the class/instance line honest: the durable shared thing is the self-documenting name (`cairn compile` reads in any ticket or doc); the fingers-preference form is per-instance personal data. Minimalism (Law 1): the dispatcher is the smallest thing that resolves-and-hands-off — resolution smarts grow against a real need, not before.
invoke: Run: `bin/cairn <name> <args...>` (or `cairn <name> ...` once bin/ is on PATH — see host_seam). No args or an unknown name prints the command list and fails non-zero. Add a verb: drop an executable at `bin/cmd/<name>`. Override the command dir (for the proof): `CAIRN_CMD_DIR=<dir>`. Prove: `python3 bin/proofs/test_cairn_command.py` (exit 0 = green).
node_class: code-seam — the dispatcher script + bin/cmd/ tree are the code (git-tracked, one address). Carries a host-seam EDGE: being callable as bare `cairn` from anywhere needs bin/ on PATH, which lives where git cannot see it. This is ticket cairn-command.

host_seam.what: making `cairn` resolve from any directory — the PATH wiring, host-side, not in git
host_seam.apply: add the repo's bin/ to PATH, e.g. append to ~/.bashrc: `export PATH="$HOME/dev/src/cairn/bin:$PATH"` (or a drop-in under /etc/prof.d). A personal shorthand is optional and also host-side: `alias cn=cairn`.
host_seam.verify: `command -v cairn` resolves into <repo>/bin/cairn, and `cairn compile` runs. Re-run after any shell/profile change — the seal expires when the host drifts (nothing in git changes).
host_seam.state: IOU — not yet applied on this host; bin/cairn is invoked by relative path today. Apply when bare `cairn` is wanted from outside the repo.

entry: FRESH build 2026-07-23. The metaphor is quarried from UU (a `uu` script-prefix dispatcher — `$UU_HOME/scripts/$1 $2 ...`); the shape is Cairn's: a dumb name-resolver that owns nothing, loud on the unknown, argv+exit-code transparent, with the full name in git and the shorthand in instance-space. What is NEW: the class/instance split of the name itself (the design decision the /intent's prefix question resolved into), and the exit-code-transparency contract (a subcommand's failure IS the dispatcher's failure).
state_and_history: compiled to state.json (projected window) + history.json (append-only voyage) beside this charter — the charter-state-history split; this file stays pure intention+why, the operational voyage lives in the sidecars (Law 1/4/7)
filed_edges: (a) the flat bin/cmd/<name> passthrough has no subcommand tree / arg-routing — grows against the first facility that needs it
(b) the PATH host-seam is not yet applied on this host (invoked by relative path today)
(c) the charter is not yet gathered by the compiler — gather scans cairn/*/ and a repo-root bin/ charter is outside that scan, closed when seed-claim coverage demands it
gates: proof gate — proofs/test_cairn_command.py, runnable bare (python3) and under the tester. Host-seam gate: the apply/verify recipe above (re-runnable; the seal expires on host drift). Durability gate: not done until proven AND the VALIDATION is persisted beside-code AND committed (git).
falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) argv is not passed verbatim (an arg is dropped, re-split, or reordered); (2) the subcommand's exit code is NOT the dispatcher's exit code (a non-zero swallowed — the exact bug found at build); (3) an unknown name does not fail loud (a silent no-op, or a zero exit — CP1 broken); (4) the dispatcher adds output of its own on the success path (it became a wrapper, not a pass-through); (5) the dispatcher grows knowledge of WHAT a subcommand does (special-casing a facility — Law 6, it must only resolve-and-hand-off); (6) the 2-letter shorthand reappears baked into the git tree (an instance concern crossing into class-space).
how_it_learns: Yes — narrowly, and it says so. The dispatcher itself does not learn (it is a dumb resolver, by design); what LEARNS is the command SET it exposes — every `bin/cmd/<name>` that appears is evidence of a facility Akien reached for often enough to name, and the growth of that directory over time is the record of which routes earned a front door. The dispatcher's own horizon (flat passthrough vs a subcommand tree) firms up as that set grows and a real facility needs argument-routing — a guess-with-an-expiry, not a settled shape.
traces_to: founding intention (get Akien out of running the system — a named route, not a memorized path)
Law 1 (the resolver is not spent re-typing settled paths — the route is compiled into a name; the dispatcher is the minimal thing that resolves-and-hands-off)
Law 6 (the dispatcher owns nothing it runs — it resolves a name and hands off to that facility's own gate)
the roots table (class-space is shareable/git; the ergonomic shorthand is instance-space personal data, never in git)
owner: CC owns the cairn-command code (bin/cairn + bin/cmd/) + its proof and gates writes to them. The dispatcher owns no facility state — each subcommand's behavior is owned by that facility (Law 6). The PATH host-seam is owned host-side (instance-space), applied and verified by its recipe, never in git.
proof: proofs/test_cairn_command.py — four teeth a hollow dispatcher could not pass: args reach the subcommand verbatim (a space-bearing arg not re-split); the subcommand's exit code propagates; an unknown name fails loud with a legible message; no wrapper noise on the success path. Uses CAIRN_CMD_DIR to point at a temp stub tree so it never touches the real bin/cmd/. Green bare (python3) and under the tester; VALIDATION persisted beside-code at validations/test_cairn_command.json.
provenance: Born 2026-07-23, cast (ticket cairn-command) and built the same session, from an /intent that bundled a command-runner + an intentions updater + a 'repo-scanner'. The prefix question (cc/cs/cr/ccs) resolved by MEASURING PATH conflicts (only `cc` taken — the C compiler, which already means CC-the-mind) and by placing the ergonomic shorthand on the correct side of the class/instance line: full `cairn` in git, personal alias in dotfiles. Akien: 'i thought we'd already decided cairn as the name'; 'let's not do a shortening at this time. cairn is not very long.' The set -e exit-code bug was found by the proof at build and fixed before the seal.
